evals.prompt: I need to simulate a call center where calls arrive randomly and wait for one of 3 agents. I want average wait time and agent utilization over an 8-hour shift.
evals.expected_output: Invokes alterlab-simpy: builds a discrete-event model with a simpy.Environment, a simpy.Resource(capacity=3) for the agents, a customer generator with randomized inter-arrival and service times (with a seeded random), uses 'with resource.request() as req:' context managers, runs env.run(until=...), and reports average wait time and resource utilization via a ResourceMonitor.

assertions.value: Models agents as a SimPy Resource, collects per-customer wait times, and reports average wait time and utilization.

evals.prompt: Model a machine shop where rush orders should be able to interrupt normal jobs already on a machine. I want to see how preemption affects throughput.
evals.expected_output: Invokes alterlab-simpy: uses a simpy.PreemptiveResource so high-priority (rush) requests can interrupt lower-priority jobs mid-service, models the interrupt handling in the process generator, runs the simulation, and reports throughput differences with monitoring.

evals.prompt: Simulate a warehouse where a producer adds finished items to a buffer and a consumer pulls them out on a different schedule, so I can see when the buffer fills up or empties.
evals.expected_output: Invokes alterlab-simpy: implements a producer-consumer pattern using a simpy.Store as the shared buffer, with producer (store.put) and consumer (store.get) generator processes on different timeouts, runs env.run(until=...), and tracks buffer level over time.

assertions.value: Uses a SimPy Store as a shared buffer with put/get processes and tracks buffer occupancy over simulated time.

evals.prompt: For our clinic, I want to find the minimum number of nurses needed so that 95% of patients wait less than 10 minutes. Can you run the simulation across different staffing levels?
evals.expected_output: Invokes alterlab-simpy: parameterizes nurse count as a Resource capacity, runs the queue simulation across multiple staffing levels (with seeded reproducibility), collects wait-time distributions per level, and identifies the minimum capacity meeting the 95%-under-10-minutes service target.

evals.prompt: I have observed inter-arrival times and service times from my call center logs. Fit an M/M/c queueing model and estimate the parameters analytically with confidence intervals.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT invoke this skill; defers to a classical statistical modeling skill (alterlab-statsmodels). The user wants analytical parameter estimation with confidence intervals from observed data, not a process-based discrete-event simulation of resource contention.

evals.prompt: Find the staffing configuration across five departments that simultaneously minimizes total labor cost and minimizes average customer wait, giving me the Pareto front of trade-offs.
evals.expected_output: Does NOT invoke this skill; defers to alterlab-pymoo. The user wants multi-objective optimization and a Pareto front of competing objectives, which is pymoo's territory, not a single discrete-event simulation run.
